question_title: Will the interest in “scott jennings” change between 2026-05-05 and 2026-05-16 according to Google Trends?
summary: Scott Jennings had a massive viral F-bomb incident on CNN on April 30, 2026, generating enormous search interest. May 5 (the start comparison date) is only 5 days after this spike, with interest still clearly elevated. May 16 (the end date) is 16 days post-spike, well past typical attention decay windows. Academic research shows 90-97% of spike interest evaporates within one week. Monte Carlo simulations across multiple decay scenarios consistently yield 80-96% probability of Decreases. The ±3 threshold is narrow relative to the expected 15-35 point decline. The main risk to Decreases is CNN firing/suspending Jennings (active pressure campaign), which could create a new spike elevating May 16 above May 5, especially with Google Trends rescaling effects. However, even with an 18% new-event probability, simulations still heavily favor Decreases.

factors.description: May 5 is only 5 days after the massive viral F-bomb incident (April 30). Interest is clearly still elevated at ~40-60 on the Google Trends scale within the resolution window.
May 16 is 16 days after the April 30 spike. Research shows 90-97% of spike interest evaporates within 1 week. Even with sustained controversy, the decay over 16 days is substantial.
The ±3 threshold is narrow relative to the expected 15-35 point decline. Even with slow decay (half-life 10 days), the difference would be ~30-40 points.

Active pressure campaign to fire/suspend Jennings from CNN. If CNN takes action between May 5-16, this creates a new spike potentially elevating May 16 above May 5. ~15-18% probability of a major new event.
Jennings continues on CNN regularly; another viral moment or escalation could spike interest. The spike would need to be timed near May 16 specifically.
Google Trends rescaling: if a new event bigger than April 30 spike occurs, it rescales the entire window, compressing May 5 and potentially elevating May 16.

reasoning:
**Forecast Summary:**

| Outcome | Probability |
|---------|-------------|
| **Decreases** | **82%** |
| Increases | 12% |
| Doesn't change | 6% |

**Key reasoning:** Scott Jennings's April 30 F-bomb on CNN live TV generated a massive viral spike in search interest. May 5 (the start date) is just 5 days post-spike — interest is still highly elevated. May 16 (the end date) is 16 days post-spike, well past typical attention decay windows where 90-97% of spike interest evaporates. Monte Carlo simulations consistently project a 15-35 point decline, far exceeding the ±3 "doesn't change" threshold.

The main upside risk (12% for Increases) comes from the active pressure campaign to fire Jennings from CNN — if CNN takes action near May 16, the resulting new spike could elevate that date's value above May 5, especially with Google Trends rescaling effects.

resolution_criteria: This question resolves “Doesn't change” if the value on the timeline at [https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=scott%20jennings&date=2026-04-16%202026-05-16](https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=scott%20jennings&date=2026-04-16%202026-05-16) for 2026-05-16 is within 3 of the value at 2026-05-05. It resolves “Increases” if the value is more than 3 greater, and resolves “Decreases” if the value is more than 3 lower.
fine_print:
A script will be used to determine the resolution of this question. It will access the data on Google Trends using [SerpApi](https://serpapi.com/), and compare the height of the timeline for the two aforementioned dates. The specific python query will be structured as follows:
```
from serpapi import GoogleSearch

search = GoogleSearch(params={
  "api_key": API_KEY,
  "engine": "google_trends",
  "data_type": "TIMESERIES",
  "geo": "US",
  "tz": 0,
  "q": "scott jennings",
  "date": "2026-04-16 2026-05-16",
})
search.get_dict()
```
Note that there may be differences between the results returned by the API and the data appearing on the page. This seems to be due to the 'tz' parameter not having the intended effect in-browser. In this case, the API results will be considered authoritative.
Note that the precision of the timeline will be 1 day, so this will compare the overall interest for the whole day as determined by Google Trends.
Dates are determined in UTC.
If the data is no longer available, or the script fails, this question will be annulled or manually resolved by a moderator.

factors.description: May 5 is only 5 days after the massive viral F-bomb incident (April 30). Interest is clearly still elevated. Median estimate ~40-60 on the Google Trends scale within the resolution window.

May 16 is 16 days after the April 30 spike. Research shows 90-97% of spike interest evaporates within 1 week for one-time events. Even with sustained controversy, the decay over 16 days is substantial.
The ±3 threshold is narrow relative to the expected 15-35 point decline. Even with slow decay (half-life 10 days), the difference would be ~30-40 points.

Active pressure campaign to fire/suspend Jennings from CNN. If CNN takes action between May 5-16, this would create a new spike that could push May 16 values higher than May 5. ~10-15% probability of this happening.
Jennings continues to appear on CNN regularly. Another viral moment or escalation of the controversy could spike interest. However, the spike would need to be timed near May 16 specifically.
Google Trends rescaling: if a new event bigger than April 30 spike occurs, it rescales the entire window, potentially compressing the May 5 value and elevating May 16.

input.anchor: Base rate for Google Trends questions with recently elevated interest (within 5 days of major viral event): ~80-85% Decreases over an 11-day forward window, based on academic literature on attention decay (Lorenz-Spreen et al. 2019, Cook et al. 2012).
input.assessment:
Scott Jennings had a massive viral moment on April 30 (F-bomb on CNN live TV), which was 5 days before the start comparison date (May 5). The controversy is ongoing as of May 4-5 with calls for his firing, media coverage, and Jennings's public responses.

The key dynamic: May 5 is still in the spike's elevated phase (estimated ~40-60 on the Google Trends scale where April 30 = 100). May 16 is 16 days post-spike, well past typical decay periods. Academic research shows 90-97% of spike interest evaporates within one week.

Monte Carlo simulations across multiple scenarios (varying decay rates, new event probabilities, noise levels) consistently yield 80-96% probability of "Decreases." The main uncertainty is whether a major new event (CNN fires/suspends Jennings, new viral moment) occurs near enough to May 16 to elevate that date's value above May 5.

The "Doesn't change" outcome requires May 16 to be within 3 points of May 5, which is very unlikely given the expected 15-35 point gap between an elevated day 5 post-spike and a near-baseline day 16 post-spike.

My previous prediction on a similar Thom Tillis Google Trends question (predicted 45% Decreases, resolved "Doesn't change") provides some calibration, but that situation was fundamentally different - Tillis had a much lower starting value (24 vs 63) with less dramatic recent activity. The Jennings case has a much stronger spike-decay signal.
input.calibration_notes: Monte Carlo gives 96% Decreases but I discount to 80% for model uncertainty, unknown unknowns, and calibration from my Tillis miss. The 13% Increases reflects genuine possibility of CNN action (firing/suspension) or new viral moment. The 7% Doesn't change is kept low because the expected gap is 15-35 points, far exceeding the ±3 threshold.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. Whether CNN takes action (fires/suspends Jennings) - this would create a new spike. 2. Whether another viral moment occurs. 3. The actual decay rate (observed 63 at day 2 suggests half-life ~3 days, but ongoing controversy may slow it). 4. Google Trends normalization within the Apr 16 - May 16 resolution window.
input.tool_audit: Web search: useful - found extensive coverage of the F-bomb incident and ongoing controversy. Google Trends direct fetch: failed (requires JavaScript rendering). Research sub-agent: partially useful - couldn't access current news but provided excellent academic background on attention decay patterns. Monte Carlo simulations: very useful, ran multiple scenarios. Metaculus question fetch: useful for reading resolution criteria and finding similar past questions. Past prediction on Tillis: useful calibration data point.
input.update_triggers: CNN fires or suspends Jennings (big shift toward Increases, especially if near May 16). Jennings leaves CNN voluntarily. New viral on-air incident. Iran war escalation making Jennings a recurring top-searched commentator.
input.process_reflection: The Google Trends direct URL fetch failed as expected (requires JS rendering). The research sub-agent's web tools were unavailable for current news but its academic knowledge base was surprisingly rich. The SerpApi tool that actually resolves these questions isn't available to me, so I'm relying on modeling the dynamics. The simulation framework worked well for this type of question. Would benefit from having historical resolution data for these Google Trends questions in a structured format.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Checks Performed

### Resolution Criteria — PASS
Resolution criteria are fully provided and correctly engaged. The agent correctly identifies: comparing May 5 vs May 16 values in the April 16–May 16 Google Trends window, ±3 threshold for "Doesn't change," SerpAPI as authoritative source, daily precision, UTC dates. The agent fetched the full question details via `get_metaculus_questions` (post_id 43408) early in the trace.

### Hallucinated Evidence — PASS
All six factors are grounded in the research trace:

- **Factor 1** (May 5 is 5 days after the F-bomb incident, interest still elevated): The web search results confirm the April 30 F-bomb incident extensively (Daily Beast, HuffPost, Barrett Media, Poynter, Variety, etc.). The question background states the May 2 value was 63. The "~40-60 on the Google Trends scale" estimate is derived from the agent's exponential decay model (execute_code output showing day 5 ≈ 31.5 from pure decay, plus baseline), which is reasonable. ✓

- **Factor 2** (90-97% of spike interest evaporates within 1 week): This comes directly from the research sub-agent's output at lines 105-106 of the trace: "90–97% gone within 1 week for a one-time event." The sources are Lorenz-Spreen et al. (2019) and empirical case studies. ✓

- **Factor 3** (±3 threshold is narrow relative to expected 15-35 point decline): The Monte Carlo simulations in the trace show median difference of -32.6 (refined simulation), supporting the 15-35+ point expected gap. ✓

- **Factor 4** (CNN firing/suspension pressure campaign): Multiple web search results confirm Jim Acosta, Keith Olbermann, and others calling for Jennings's firing. The pressure campaign is well-documented (Atlanta Black Star article about critics giving CNN "a deadline to act"). ✓

- **Factor 5** (Jennings continues to appear on CNN, possible new viral moment): Barrett Media confirms Jennings addressed the situation publicly and continues on CNN. ✓

- **Factor 6** (Google Trends rescaling): This is a well-understood property of Google Trends documented in both the question background and the research sub-agent's output about RSV normalization. ✓

### Double-Counting — MINOR (no action needed)
Factors 1 and 2 draw on overlapping evidence — both reference the April 30 spike and the decay pattern. Factor 1 says "still elevated" on May 5; Factor 2 says "90-97% gone within 1 week." They address different aspects (current state vs. decay rate) but the underlying evidence is the same spike-decay dynamic. Factor 3 also builds on the same evidence (the expected decline magnitude from Factors 1-2 applied to the threshold). However, since the agent's final estimate (80% Decreases) is well below the factor-implied probability (~98.5%), this double-counting is not inflating the submitted probability.

### Wrong-Direction Factors — PASS
All logit signs match their descriptions:
- Factors 1-3 support "Decreases" with positive logits ✓
- Factors 4-6 support "Increases" with positive logits ✓

### Contradictory Assessment — PASS
The assessment concludes 80% Decreases, consistent with factors overwhelmingly favoring that outcome. The agent explicitly acknowledges the gap between factor-implied (98.5%) and submitted (80%) probability, explaining it as deliberate conservatism.

### Probability Assessment — NO SIGNIFICANT CONCERN
The agent's Monte Carlo simulations consistently show ~95-96% for Decreases. The agent discounts to 80%, citing model uncertainty, Thom Tillis calibration miss, and new event possibility.

The ~16pp discount from 96% to 80% is larger than in prior Google Trends forecasts (e.g., Swalwell: 96%→92%; British Royal Family: 99%→92%). The agent justifies this larger discount partly through the Tillis calibration miss, though as the agent itself notes, the Tillis situation was fundamentally different (starting value 24 vs 63, 7-day window vs 11-day).

My independent assessment: The spike decay signal here is very strong. May 5 is day 5 post-spike (still substantially elevated, estimated 30-50 in the resolution window). May 16 is day 16 (near baseline, estimated 5-15). The expected gap of 15-35 points far exceeds the ±3 threshold. The main risk is a new event (CNN firing Jennings) creating a counter-spike near May 16 — the agent assigns ~15-18% to this, which seems reasonable given the active pressure campaign. My estimate: ~83-87% Decreases, ~8-12% Increases, ~3-5% Doesn't change.

The agent's 80% is about 3-7pp below my range, reflecting excessive conservatism but not enough to constitute a fail. The directional forecast is clearly correct, and the ~7pp gap from my central estimate is modest. The agent's conservatism pattern is consistent with its behavior on prior Google Trends questions (always discounting 7-16pp below simulations).

### Anchor Divergence — PASS
Anchor is 80-85%. Final probability is 80%, at the low end of the anchor range. This is consistent — the agent is not departing from its anchor, just sitting at its conservative edge.

### Weak Counterargument — PASS
The counterargument is strong and substantive:
1. Identifies specific mechanism (CNN fires/suspends Jennings) with named actors (Acosta, Olbermann) and consequences (rescaling effect)
2. Challenges the spike-decay model's applicability (multi-wave controversy vs. single event)
3. References the Tillis calibration miss as evidence of systematic over-prediction
4. Notes the Iran war context as a sustaining factor
5. Provides specific thresholds that would change the estimate
This is a genuine adversarial exercise, not a token gesture.

### Overconfident Self-Assessment — PASS
Confidence at 0.55 is appropriate given: (1) inability to access actual Google Trends data for this specific term, (2) genuine uncertainty about new events in the 11-day window, (3) the Tillis calibration miss (though different situation), (4) reliance on inferred decay models rather than observed data.

### Tool Audit — PASS
Agent claims: "Google Trends direct fetch: failed (requires JavaScript rendering)." The fetch_url tool returned successfully (1 call, 0 errors per metrics) but with content saying "browser being used is unsupported" — this is confirmed in the trace at lines 354-359. The agent's characterization is functionally accurate (it was a usefulness failure, not a tool error). All other tool audit claims match the ground truth metrics (13 calls, 0 errors).

### Worldview Consistency — PASS
The worldview research entries (scott-jennings-cnn-news and google-trends-patterns-for-political-commentators) are consistent with the factors. The Scott Jennings research entry acknowledges it couldn't access May 2026 news but provides background. The Google Trends patterns research provides the decay statistics used in the factors. No contradictions found.

### Pre-Publication Event — N/A
This is a forward-looking question comparing two future dates (May 5 vs May 16).

### Binary vs. Numeric Consistency — N/A
No numeric sub-forecast found for this question.

## Summary
The forecast is well-constructed with thorough evidence. The direction (Decreases) is overwhelmingly supported by the viral F-bomb incident decay dynamics and multiple Monte Carlo simulations. All factual claims are grounded in the research trace. The 80% probability for Decreases is somewhat conservative relative to the simulation evidence (~96%) and my independent estimate (~85%), but the gap is modest (~5pp from my estimate) and the agent's conservatism is deliberate and explained. No hallucinated evidence, no wrong-direction factors, no contradictory assessment, and resolution criteria are properly engaged. The counterargument is strong and the self-confidence is appropriately calibrated.
